attributes.stem:
A 76-year-old male presents with a two month history of painless double vision. It is generally better in the morning and worsens throughout the day. His symptoms have progressed over the last two weeks. Additionally, he needs to hold his eyes open with his hands in order to read a book. He has no facial, arm or leg weakness and can walk and swallow normally. He has no headaches.

On examination, he is alert and oriented. He has bilateral ptosis with no gaze palsy or ophthalmoplegia. There is no lid retraction or proptosis. His pupillary reflex is intact bilaterally. There is no relative afferent pupillary defect. His facial movements have normal power bilaterally. He has no bulbar palsy, no hoarse voice, and an intact gag reflex.
attributes.stemQuestion: Given the most likely diagnosis, which one of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This presentation is characteristic of ocular myasthenia gravis. Myasthenia gravis (MG) is an autoimmune disorder affecting the neuromuscular junction (NMJ) and has two subtypes - ocular or generalised. MG is most commonly due to autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors (AchR-MG) in the post-synaptic membrane of the neuromuscular junction. A minority of cases are caused by autoantibodies against muscle specific tyrosine kinase (MuSK-MG). Antibody type helps define phenotypic variant and subgroup of MG. The course of MG begins as episodic symptoms that gradually worsens and becomes persistent.

The hallmark of MG is fatigueable muscle weakness that worsens with exertion and improves with rest. This may occur in any muscle, but the extraocular muscles are usually most frequently affected, which manifests as diplopia, ptosis and blurred vision.

  • Ocular myasthenia gravis is isolated extra ocular involvement for at least 2 years.

  • Generalised myasthenia gravis is weakness affecting muscles other than solely ocular muscles. Generalised MG commonly includes weakness of the oropharyngeal or facial muscles. Oropharyngeal muscle weakness presents as dysphagia and dysarthria and facial weakness can result in an 'expressionless' patient. Less commonly, proximal limb weakness which may be displayed as difficulty rising from a chair or climbing stairs, is present <5% of patients.

In women, MG displays a bimodal distribution, peaking at 30 and 50 years. Women are more frequently affected after the age of 40 years. In men, the incidence of MG increases with age - men are more frequently affected after 50 years, and the highest rates occur in the 60-89 age group.

It is also important to note that MG may manifest as a paraneoplastic syndrome associated with a thymoma. Thymoma MG occurs due to a humoral immune response to the epitopes on the neoplastic thymoma cells that bear resemblance to epitopes on components of the neuromuscular junction. This results in T cell expression of epitopes cross-reacting with acetylcholine receptors. All patients with a thymoma MG have AChR antibodies in their serum. Non-AChR autoantibodies, such as anti-striated muscle antibodies, are also seen in 90% of patients with thymoma MG. Thymectomy is recommended for patients with a thymoma causing MG. It is presumptuous to assume this patient has a thymoma, however, this should be screened for with a chest CT scan.

Pyridostigmine is an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor which results in decreased breakdown of acetylcholine thus prolonging the action of the neurotransmitter in the NMJ and alleviating the symptoms of MG. It is the first line management for mild generalised MG or ocular myasthenia. Given this patient has extraocular muscle weakness, pyridostigmine is the most appropriate treatment.

Prednisolone and azathioprine are first line drugs for immunosuppression treatment in MG. The indications are moderate-severe MG and incomplete response to pyridostigmine. This patient does not display signs of moderate-severe MG and has not been initiated on first line anticholinergic therapy with pyridostigmine, therefore this answer is incorrect.

Methotrexate (MTX) can be used as an alternative corticosteroid sparing drug as treatment for MG. A 2011 randomised controlled trial compared to methotrexate in 24 patients with generalised MG with azathioprine regarding the steroid sparing effect and showed an equivalent effect within treatment period of 2 years. A 2016 publication in the Journal of Neurology states MTX with folic acid can therefore be regarded as a second line treatment for MG. It is important to note MTX displays significant side effects of MTX such as hepatotoxicity, leukopenia, acute pneumonitis, kidney failure etc., This is incorrect as methotrexate is not the first line treatment for mild MG.

Propantheline is an anticholinergic drug that can be used in conjunction with an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (e.g., pyridostigmine) to control the muscarinic adverse effects such as diarrhoea, abdominal pain, cramps, exacerbation of glaucoma. However, this patient is yet to be started on pyridostigmine so it is presumptuous to assume they will have side effects. Therefore this answer is incorrect. 

In terms of other diagnoses, this case is unlikely to be Lambert Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) which is commonly associated with small cell lung carcinoma due to absent pulmonary symptoms, no muscle weakness anywhere other than eyes (ocular involvement is less common in LEMS and proximal limb weakness is common), and no autonomic symptoms (dry mouth, constipation). The absence of lid lag, lid retraction, proptosis and periorbital oedema makes thyroid eye disease less likely to be the cause. Intact pupillary reflex and normal sized pupils and ocular alignment makes oculomotor nerve palsy unlikely as well.
learningPoints: Extraocular muscle weakness is the most common initial symptom in myasthenia gravis
Myasthenia gravis is characterised by muscle fatigueability that improves with rest
references: Melzer, N., Ruck, T., Fuhr, P. et al. Clinical features, pathogenesis, and treatment of myasthenia gravis: a supplement to the Guidelines of the German Neurological Society. J Neurol 263, 1473–1494 (2016). https://doi.org/10.1007/s00415-016-8045-z
Dresser L, Wlodarski R, Rezania K, Soliven B. Myasthenia gravis: epidemiology, pathophysiology and clinical manifestations. Journal of clinical medicine. 2021 May 21;10(11):2235.
Ruff RL, Lisak RP. Nature and action of antibodies in myasthenia gravis. Neurologic clinics. 2018 May 1;36(2):275-91.
Thanvi, B. R., & Lo, T. C. N. (2004). Update on myasthenia gravis. Postgraduate Medical Journal, 80(950), 690. https://doi.org/10.1136/pgmj.2003.017483
